subject: Physics
q: The velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$) plot of a particle is shown in the figure, for a time interval of $40\text{ s}$. The total distance travelled by the particle and the average velocity during this period are, respectively _______.
question: The velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$) plot of a particle is shown in the figure, for a time interval of $40\text{ s}$. The total distance travelled by the particle and the average velocity during this period are, respectively _______.
solution:
The total distance travelled by the particle is equal to the sum of the magnitudes of the areas under the velocity-time graph.

Area of the first triangle (from $t = 0$ to $t = 20\text{ s}$) is:
$A_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5 = 50\text{ m}$

Magnitude of the area of the second triangle (from $t = 20$ to $t = 40\text{ s}$) is:
$|A_2| = \left| \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times (-5) \right| = 50\text{ m}$

Total distance $= A_1 + |A_2| = 50 + 50 = 100\text{ m}$

The total displacement is the algebraic sum of the areas under the velocity-time graph.

Total displacement $= A_1 + A_2 = 50 - 50 = 0\text{ m}$

Average velocity is the ratio of total displacement to total time.

Average velocity $= \dfrac{\text{Total displacement}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{0}{40} = 0$

Answer: $100\text{ m}$ and zero

subject: Physics
q: An automobile travelling with speed of $60 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can brake to stop within a distance of $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the car is going twice as fast, i.e $120 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the stopping distance will be
question: An automobile travelling with speed of $60 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can brake to stop within a distance of $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the car is going twice as fast, i.e $120 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the stopping distance will be
solution: If the initial speed is doubled, the stopping distance becomes four times, i.e. $80 \mathrm{~m}$.

subject: Physics
q: The velocity-time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) the difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in $15 \mathrm{~s}$ and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively
question: The velocity-time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) the difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in $15 \mathrm{~s}$ and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively
solution:
Using equation, $a=\frac{v-u}{t}$ and
$$
S=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2
$$
Distance travelled by car in $15 \mathrm{sec}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(45)}{15}(15)^2$
$=\frac{675}{2} \mathrm{~m}$
Distance traveled by scooter in 15 seconds $=30 \times 15=450(\because$ distance $=$ speed $\times$ time $)$ Difference between distance travelled by car and scooter in $15 \mathrm{sec}, 450-337.5=112.5 \mathrm{~m}$ Let car catches scooter in time t;
$\frac{675}{2}+45(t-15)=30 t$
$337.5+45 \mathrm{t}-675=30 \mathrm{t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 15 \mathrm{t}=337.5$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{t}=22.5 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q: A person travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average velocity of the person is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>, then the relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be
question: A person travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> distance with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average velocity of the person is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>, then the relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtable columnalign="right left right left right left right left right left right left" columnspacing="0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em 2em 0em" rowspacing="3pt"><mtr><mtd><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>
solution:
The time t1 taken by the person to travel distance x with velocity v1 is given by

t1=xv1   ...1

The time t2 taken by the person to travel distance x with velocity v2 is given by

t2=xv2   ...2

The formula to calculate the average speed v of the person is given by

v=2xt1+t2   ...3

Substitute the expressions from equations (1) and (2) into equation (3) to obtain the required relation.

v=2xxv1+xv2⇒xv1+xv2=2xv⇒2v=1v1+1v2

solution:
V=αt+βt2

dsdt=αt+βt2

∫S1S2 ds=∫12αt+βt2dt

S2-S1=αt22+βt3312

As the particle is not changing direction So distance = displacement. Distance =α[4-1]2+β[8-1]3

subject: Physics
q: The co-ordinates of a moving particle at any time ' $t$ ' are given by $x=\alpha t^3$ and $y=\beta t^3$. The speed of the particle at time ' $t$ ' is given by
question: The co-ordinates of a moving particle at any time ' $t$ ' are given by $x=\alpha t^3$ and $y=\beta t^3$. The speed of the particle at time ' $t$ ' is given by
solution:
$V_x=\frac{d x}{d t}=3 \alpha t^2, V_y=\frac{d y}{d t}=3 \beta t^2$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{y}^2}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2 \sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2}$

v=u-gt

y=c+mx

m=-g = slope

c=+u

Velocity becomes negative after some time and the negative slope is constant.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving unidirectional on a horizontal plane under the action of a constant power supplying energy source. The displacement (s) – time (t) graph that describes the motion of the particle is (graphs are drawn schematically and are not to scale):
question: A particle is moving unidirectional on a horizontal plane under the action of a constant power supplying energy source. The displacement (s) – time (t) graph that describes the motion of the particle is (graphs are drawn schematically and are not to scale):
solution:
Here Power is constant,
Hence, P=Fv=mav=av=constant.

av=kvdvdx×v=k∫0vv2dv=k∫0xdx∴v=3k1/3×x1/3⇒v=Ax1/3 where A=3k1/3=constant ....1

Also, v=dxdt∴dxdt=Ax1/3⇒∫0ddxx1/3=A∫0tdt

Simplifying further we get,

⇒d=At3/2    ...2

From equations 1 & 2 its clear that the graph won't be straight line so first & third options cant be correct.
Now, from equation 1 its clear that when x is increased then the slope dsdt should also increase which is possible in second option. Hence option 2 is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is released from rest in a uniform electric field. If there is no other force on the particle, the dependence of its speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> on the distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> travelled by it is correctly given by (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is released from rest in a uniform electric field. If there is no other force on the particle, the dependence of its speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> on the distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> travelled by it is correctly given by (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
The particle is at rest initially.

So, the initial velocity u=0 m s-1.

The force on the charged particle in the uniform electric field,

F=qE

⇒ ma=qE

⇒ a=qEm

Applying the velocity-displacement equation under constant acceleration,

v2=u2+2ax

⇒ v2=02+2qEmx

⇒ v2=2qEmx

q: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
According to the question, velocity is increasing at 5 m s-1. Therefore, dvds=5 s-1. 

Now acceleration a=dvdt=vdvds

⇒a=vdvds=20×5=100 m s-2

q: A tennis ball is dropped on to the floor from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Ball comes in contact with the floor for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]

It rebounds to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Ball comes in contact with the floor for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The average acceleration during contact is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
The speed of ball just before collision with ground is vi2=0+2ghi

 ⇒vi=2ghi

=2×10×9.8

=14 m s-1  Downward

The speed of ball just after collision is 0=vf2-2ghf

⇒vf=2ghf

=2×10×5

=10 m s-1  Upward

Average acceleration of ball is a→avg=Δv→Δt=vf--vit=10+140.2=240.2=120 m s-2.

q:
A particle starts from origin O from rest and moves with a uniform acceleration along the positive  x-axis . Identify all figures that correctly represent the motion qualitatively.
( a= acceleration, v= velocity, x=  dispalcement, t= time)
A
B
(C)
(D)
question:
A particle starts from origin O from rest and moves with a uniform acceleration along the positive  x-axis . Identify all figures that correctly represent the motion qualitatively.
( a= acceleration, v= velocity, x=  dispalcement, t= time)
A
B
(C)
(D)
solution:
A. Acceleration =  constant; a v→t graph will be constant line parallel to time axis. Option A is correct.
B. v=u+at ; velocity vs time graph will be straight line passing through origin. Option B is correct.
u=0;  initially at rest
v= at
C. X=12at2 (particle was initially at origin) v→t graph will be parabola passing through origin. Option D is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A car covers AB distance with first one-third at velocity v1 m s-1, second one-third at v2 m s-1 and last one-third at v3 m s-1. If v3=3v1,v2=2v1 and v1=11 m s-1, then the average velocity of the car is _____  m s-1.
question: A car covers AB distance with first one-third at velocity v1 m s-1, second one-third at v2 m s-1 and last one-third at v3 m s-1. If v3=3v1,v2=2v1 and v1=11 m s-1, then the average velocity of the car is _____  m s-1.
solution:
Let the total distance be d.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t1=d3v1.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t2=d3v2.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t3=d3v3.

Average speed=Total distanceTotal time

vavg=dd3v1+d3v2+d3v3

⇒vavg=3v1v2v3v1v2+v2v3+v3v1

Here, v1=11 m s-1, then, v3=33 m s-1, v2=22  m s-1

Vavg =3×11×22×3311×22+22×33+33×11=18 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
In 4th sec, 1st drop will travel 

⇒12×9.8×42=78.4 m

∴ 2nd drop would have travelled

⇒78.4-34.3=44.1 m

Time for 2nd drop

129.8t2=44.1

t=3 sec

∴ Each drop have time gap of 1 sec.

∴ 1 drop per second.

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and after applying the brake it will move <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before it stops. If the same car is moving with a speed of one third the reported speed then it will stop after travelling _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance.
question: A car is moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and after applying the brake it will move <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before it stops. If the same car is moving with a speed of one third the reported speed then it will stop after travelling _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance.
solution:
In both cases, the final velocity is zero. Using equation of motion for constant acceleration

v2=u2+2as

⇒02-u2=-2a27    ...1

⇒02-u32=-2as'     ...2

From 1 and 2,

2a27=92as'

⇒s'=3 m

subject: Physics
q: When a car is at rest, its driver sees rain drops falling on it vertically. When driving the car with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>, he sees that rain drops coming at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> from the horizontal. On further increasing the speed of the car to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi></mi></math>, this angle changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></math> is close to :
question: When a car is at rest, its driver sees rain drops falling on it vertically. When driving the car with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>, he sees that rain drops coming at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> from the horizontal. On further increasing the speed of the car to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi></mi></math>, this angle changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi></math> is close to :
solution:
tan=60°=vrgv               ........1

tan45°=vrg(β+1)v            ..........2

3v=(β+1)v

β=3-1=0.732

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xy</mi></math>-plane experiences a velocity dependent force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub></math> are the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> components of its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is the acceleration of the particle, then which of the following statements is true for the particle ?
question: A particle moving in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xy</mi></math>-plane experiences a velocity dependent force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub></math> are the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> components of its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is the acceleration of the particle, then which of the following statements is true for the particle ?
options: kinetic energy of particle is constant in time
solution:
F→=kv→
⇒ F→‖v→
⇒ a→‖v→
⇒ v→×a→=0 always

q: Velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> in two systems of units <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>n</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> respectively. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are constants. The relations for distance and time in two systems respectively are
question: Velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> in two systems of units <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>n</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> respectively. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are constants. The relations for distance and time in two systems respectively are
solution:
Distance is stated as [L]=v2[a]

 ⇒v22a2=nm2v12a1mn2=n3m3v12a1

Or, we have L2=n3m3L1

Similarly, time T=va

⇒v2a2=nm2v1a1mn=n2mv1a1

So, T2=n2mT1

subject: Physics
q: The position of a particle as a function of time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> are constants. When the particles zero acceleration, then its velocity will be:
question: The position of a particle as a function of time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> are constants. When the particles zero acceleration, then its velocity will be:
solution:
x(t)=at+bt2-ct3
velocity v(t)=ddt(x)=ddtat+bt2-ct3
=a+2bt-3ct2
Acceleration =ddtv(t)=2b-6tc
acceleration=0⇒2b-6tc=0
t=b3c
∴ velocity when t=b3c,
vt=b3c=a+2bb3c-3b3c2
=a+2b23c-b23c
=a+b23c

subject: Physics
q: An object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is thrown vertically upwards from the ground. The air resistance produces a constant retarding force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> throughout the motion. The ratio of time of ascent to the time of descent will be equal to : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].
question: An object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is thrown vertically upwards from the ground. The air resistance produces a constant retarding force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> throughout the motion. The ratio of time of ascent to the time of descent will be equal to : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].
solution:
Net force while going upward will be mg+R=50+10=60 N

⇒aup=605=12 m s-2

Similarly, Net force while going downward will be mg-R=40 N

⇒ad=405=8 m s-2

Since 

h=12at2⇒auptup2adtd2=1⇒tuptd=812=23

subject: Physics
q: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> for some time after which it decelerates at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> to come to rest. If the total time elapsed is t seconds, the total distance travelled is:
question: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> for some time after which it decelerates at a constant rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> to come to rest. If the total time elapsed is t seconds, the total distance travelled is:
solution:
v0=αt1 and 0=v0-βt2⇒v0=βt2

t1+t2=t

v01α+1β=t

⇒v0=αβtα+β

Distance = area of v-t graph

=12×t×v0=12×t×αβtα+β=αβt22(α+β)

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is subjected to an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction, where a and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. Initially the particle was at rest at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Other than the initial position the kinetic energy of the particle becomes zero when the distance of the particle from the origin is :
question: A particle of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is subjected to an electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>–</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction, where a and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. Initially the particle was at rest at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Other than the initial position the kinetic energy of the particle becomes zero when the distance of the particle from the origin is :
solution:
Wex=ΔK       K1-K1=0

∫0xqEdx=0

 q ∫0xE01-Qx2dx=0

qE0∫0x1-ax2dx=0

x-ax33=0

1-ax23=0

ax23=1

x2=3a

x=3a

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
question: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
solution:
u=2gh
Now,

S=h3, a=-g

S=ut+12at2
h3=2ght+12-gt2
t2g2-2ght+h3=0

From quadratic equation

t1, t2=2gh±2gh-4 g2h3 g

t1t2=2gh-4gh32gh+4gh3

=3-23+2

subject: Physics
q: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle moving with a constant acceleration can be represented by :
question: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle moving with a constant acceleration can be represented by :
solution: Represent correct graph for a particle moving with constant acceleration, as for constant acceleration velocity-time graph is a straight line with positive slope, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>t</mi></math> graph should be an opening upward parabola.

subject: Physics
q: The distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> covered by a paritcle in one dimensional motion varies with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> . If the acceleration of the particle depends on <math><mi>x</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></msup></math> , where <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer, the value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is _________
question: The distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> covered by a paritcle in one dimensional motion varies with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> . If the acceleration of the particle depends on <math><mi>x</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></msup></math> , where <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer, the value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is _________
solution:
x2=at2+2bt+c
2xv=2at+2b
xv=at+b
v2+ax=a
ax=a-at+bx2
a=aat2+2bt+c-at+b2x3
a=ac-2x3
a α x-3
